New York City BnB Analysis

Author

Jokobus

Published

July 5, 2026

1. Executive Summary

This project analyzes Bead and Breakfast pricing in New York City through exploratory data analysis, geospatial visualization, and predictive modeling. The goal is to understand which market features shape nightly rates, where the highest and lowest price concentrations occur, and how accurately those prices can be predicted from listing metadata.

Several strong patterns emerge quickly. Manhattan dominates the upper end of the market, especially south of Central Park, while lower-priced listings are more broadly distributed across the outer boroughs. Room type, location, and listing structure all contribute meaningfully to price variation, and the dataset also shows a clear $999 ceiling that likely reflects a preprocessing cap rather than a true market boundary.

The modeling section compares baseline linear regression, decision trees, and ensemble methods. The Random Forest model performs best on the holdout set and is therefore used for final prediction on the external evaluation file.

What This Report Covers

  • neighborhood and borough-level price structure
  • luxury and budget listing distributions
  • local clustering around major Manhattan landmarks
  • missing-value handling and kNN imputation checks
  • model comparison and evaluation-set prediction

2. Setup and Data Loading

In this section, we set the system language to English and load all the required packages for spatial mapping, data wrangling, visualization, and machine learning.

Code 
# Set language
Sys.setenv(LANG = "en")

# Define and load dependencies
required_packages <- c(
  "dplyr", "ggplot2", "leaflet", "randomForest", 
  "caret", "e1071", "rpart", "rpart.plot", 
  "doParallel", "caTools", "flexclust"
)

invisible(lapply(required_packages, library, character.only = TRUE))

# Load dataset
bnb <- read.csv("bnb_data.csv")
head(bnb)
        id                                 name   host_id
1 40560656 The Hoxton, Williamsburg - Cosy Room 273324213

6 42065545                      Carvi Hotel NYC 310429455

2                 The James NoMad  6/13/2019            Midtown
3 Gardens Suites Hotel By Affinia  6/17/2019    Upper East Side
4           Mint House At 70 Pine  6/21/2018 Financial District
5                  Broadway Plaza  11/6/2019            Midtown
6                     Carvi Hotel 11/18/2019            Midtown
  neighbourhood_group latitude longitude  room_type accommodates bathrooms_text
1            Brooklyn 40.72096 -73.95860 Hotel room            2               
2           Manhattan 40.74459 -73.98574 Hotel room            4

6       NA   NA     0                 0

3. Data Analysis and Visualization

3.1 Which borough has the most rentals?

The most rental listings are in the Bedford-Stuyvesant neighborhood, which contains 2,920 listings in the dataset.

At the borough level, Manhattan has the largest concentration of listings (16,632), followed closely by Brooklyn (15,571). Queens, the Bronx, and Staten Island together account for fewer than 9,000 listings, which already hints at a strong concentration of supply in the city’s core.

Code 
# Listings count by neighborhood
bnb %>% 
  group_by(neighbourhood) %>% 
  count() %>% 
  arrange(desc(n)) %>% 
  head()
# A tibble: 6 × 2
# Groups:   neighbourhood [6]
  neighbourhood          n
  <chr>              <int>
1 Bedford-Stuyvesant  2920
2 Williamsburg        2542
3 Harlem              1936
4 Bushwick            1747
5 Midtown             1725
6 Hell's Kitchen      1496
Code 
# Listings count by neighborhood group
bnb %>% 
  group_by(neighbourhood_group) %>% 
  count() %>% 
  arrange(desc(n))
# A tibble: 5 × 2
# Groups:   neighbourhood_group [5]
  neighbourhood_group     n
  <chr>               <int>
1 Manhattan           16632
2 Brooklyn            15571
3 Queens               6495
4 Bronx                1584
5 Staten Island         401
Code 
# Listing counts grouped by neighborhood and room type
bnb %>% 
  group_by(neighbourhood, room_type) %>% 
  count() %>% 
  arrange(desc(n))
# A tibble: 542 × 3
# Groups:   neighbourhood, room_type [542]
   neighbourhood      room_type           n
   <chr>              <chr>           <int>
 1 Bedford-Stuyvesant Entire home/apt  1500
 2 Williamsburg       Entire home/apt  1422
 3 Bedford-Stuyvesant Private room     1381
 4 Upper East Side    Entire home/apt  1150
 5 Midtown            Entire home/apt  1126
 6 Bushwick           Private room     1101
 7 Williamsburg       Private room     1091
 8 Harlem             Private room     1040
 9 Hell's Kitchen     Entire home/apt   998
10 Upper West Side    Entire home/apt   984
# ℹ 532 more rows

3.2 Price Analysis and Geolocation Mapping

To get a first overall impression, we analyze average prices across neighborhoods and visualize the city at several price thresholds. The static maps below use ggplot2 to make the spatial patterns easier to compare than a dense interactive layer would.

The core pattern is straightforward: expensive BnBs are concentrated in Manhattan, especially in its central and southern neighborhoods. The mean listing price is about $174, and the observed maximum is $999. Before interpreting the top end of the market, we remove listings with price $0 so that obvious anomalies do not distort the summary statistics.

Code 
# Average price per neighborhood
bnb %>% 
  group_by(neighbourhood) %>% 
  summarize(mean_price = mean(price, na.rm = TRUE))
# A tibble: 223 × 2
   neighbourhood     mean_price
   <chr>                  <dbl>
 1 Allerton                109.
 2 Arden Heights           115.
 3 Arrochar                132.
 4 Arverne                 189.
 5 Astoria                 115.
 6 Bath Beach              114.
 7 Battery Park City       250.
 8 Bay Ridge               106.
 9 Bay Terrace             158.
10 Baychester              116.
# ℹ 213 more rows
Code 
# Filter out listings with a price of 0 (Anomalies removed for standard analysis)
bnb_preproc <- bnb
bnb_0price_removed <- subset(bnb_preproc, price != 0.00)

cat(paste("**Mean price of all BnB listings:**", mean(bnb_0price_removed$price), "<br><br>"))
**Mean price of all BnB listings:** 173.638107888717 <br><br>
Code 
cat(paste("**Max price of any BnB listing:**", max(bnb_0price_removed$price), "<br><br>"))
**Max price of any BnB listing:** 999 <br><br>

3.2.1 High-End Price Thresholds Spatial Distribution

Here we examine the geographic distribution of listings at the luxury end of the market using three thresholds: $500, $900, and $999.

Code 
# Figure 1: BnBs in NYC by price
ggplot(bnb_0price_removed, aes(x = longitude, y = latitude)) + 
  geom_point(aes(color = price)) + 
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(-74.25, -73.7)) + 
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(40.5, 40.92)) + 
  scale_color_gradient(low = "gray", high = "red")
Code 
# Figure 3: BnBs with a price of $500 or more
ggplot(subset(bnb_0price_removed, price >= 500), aes(x = longitude, y = latitude)) + 
  geom_point(data = bnb_0price_removed, color = "white", alpha = 0.3) + 
  geom_point(aes(color = price)) + 
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(-74.25, -73.7)) + 
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(40.5, 40.92)) + 
  scale_color_gradient(low = "gray", high = "red")
Code 
# Figure 4: BnBs with a price of $900 or more
ggplot(subset(bnb_0price_removed, price >= 900), aes(x = longitude, y = latitude)) + 
  geom_point(data = bnb_0price_removed, color = "white", alpha = 0.3) + 
  geom_point(aes(color = price)) + 
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(-74.25, -73.7)) + 
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(40.5, 40.92)) + 
  scale_color_gradient(low = "gray", high = "red")
Code 
# Figure 5: BnBs with a price of exactly $999
ggplot(subset(bnb_0price_removed, price == 999), aes(x = longitude, y = latitude)) + 
  geom_point(data = bnb_0price_removed, color = "white", alpha = 0.3) + 
  geom_point(color = "red") + 
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(-74.25, -73.7)) + 
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(40.5, 40.92))
Code 
# Distribution tables for high tier pricing
cat(paste("**Number of listings with price >= \\$500:**", nrow(subset(bnb_0price_removed, price >= 500)), "<br><br>"))

Number of listings with price >= $500: 1754

Code 
cat(paste("**Number of listings with price >= \\$900:**", nrow(subset(bnb_0price_removed, price >= 900)), "<br><br>"))

Number of listings with price >= $900: 226

Code 
# Neighborhood group breakdowns
cat("**Neighborhood group breakdown for listings >= \\$900:**\n")

Neighborhood group breakdown for listings >= $900:

Code 
sub_900 <- subset(bnb_0price_removed, price >= 900)

# Simplify the tapply logic into a direct table count for cleaner rendering
tab_900 <- table(sub_900$neighbourhood_group)
print(knitr::kable(as.data.frame(tab_900), col.names = c("Neighborhood Group", "Count"), format = "html"))
Neighborhood Group Count
Brooklyn 36
Manhattan 184
Queens 6
Code 
cat(paste("**Number of listings with price >= \\$999:**", nrow(subset(bnb_0price_removed, price >= 999)), "<br><br>"))

Number of listings with price >= $999: 67

Code 
cat("**Neighborhood group breakdown for listings >= \\$999:**\n")

Neighborhood group breakdown for listings >= $999:

Code 
sub_999 <- subset(bnb_0price_removed, price >= 999)
tab_999 <- table(sub_999$neighbourhood_group)
print(knitr::kable(as.data.frame(tab_999), col.names = c("Neighborhood Group", "Count"), format = "html"))
Neighborhood Group Count
Brooklyn 5
Manhattan 60
Queens 2
Code 
cat(paste("**Number of listings with price >= \\$1000:**", nrow(subset(bnb_0price_removed, price >= 1000)), "<br><br>"))

Number of listings with price >= $1000: 0

Most listings above $900 are in Manhattan, particularly south of Central Park. Exactly 184 of the 226 BnBs priced above $900 are in Manhattan, which is roughly 81 percent of that luxury subset. The remainder is concentrated mainly in north-west Brooklyn, suggesting that premium demand is not exclusive to Manhattan but is still heavily centered there.

One especially notable artifact is the cluster of 67 BnBs listed at exactly $999, with none above $1,000. That pattern strongly suggests a ceiling introduced during preprocessing or listing normalization. Of those 67 listings, 60 are in Manhattan, which reinforces the idea that the top of the market is both geographically concentrated and artificially truncated.

3.2.2 Budget & Free Listings Analysis

Next we look at lower-cost options for budget-conscious travelers using a $50 cutoff.

Affordable BnBs are not limited to the outer boroughs. Even inside Manhattan, a small number of sub-$50 listings appear in highly desirable areas, although the broader concentration of budget listings expands rapidly north of Central Park and across the East River into Brooklyn. That spatial spread suggests that transit access matters almost as much as neighborhood identity for price-sensitive guests.

At the extreme low end, a few listings appear at $10, and two of them still sit within Manhattan proper. The dataset also contains a small set of $0 entries. Those values are treated as anomalies and removed from the modeling pipeline so that the downstream price prediction task reflects actual market behavior rather than data errors.

Code 
# Figure 6: BnBs with a price of $50 or less
budget_50 <- 50
ggplot(subset(bnb_0price_removed, price <= budget_50), aes(x = longitude, y = latitude)) + 
  geom_point(data = bnb_0price_removed, color = "white", alpha = 0.3) + 
  geom_point(aes(size = budget_50 - price, color = price)) + 
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(-74.25, -73.7)) + 
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(40.5, 40.92)) + 
  scale_color_gradient(low = "blue", high = "gray")
Code 
# Figure 7: BnBs with a price of $10
budget_10 <- 10
ggplot(bnb_0price_removed, aes(x = longitude, y = latitude)) + 
  geom_point(data = bnb_0price_removed, color = "white", alpha = 0.3) + 
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(-74.25, -73.7)) + 
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(40.5, 40.92)) + 
  geom_point(data = subset(bnb_0price_removed, price <= budget_10), size = 5, color = "green")
Code 
# Figure 8: Anomalous BnBs listed at $0
ggplot(bnb_preproc, aes(x = longitude, y = latitude)) + 
  geom_point(data = bnb_preproc, color = "white", alpha = 0.3) + 
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(-74.25, -73.7)) + 
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(40.5, 40.92)) + 
  geom_point(data = subset(bnb_preproc, price == 0), size = 5, color = "green")

3.2.3 Neighborhood Rankings

To identify the neighborhoods with the highest and lowest average prices, we compute neighborhood-level means after excluding very small groups with fewer than three active listings. This avoids unstable estimates from places such as New Dorp, Bull’s Head, Country Club, Fort Wadsworth, and Hollis Hills.

Table 1: Highest and lowest mean prices depending on neighborhood (rounded)
Neighborhood (High Tier) Mean Price Neighborhood (Low Tier) Mean Price
Theater District $425 West Farms $74
Tribeca $359 Grant City $61

Theater District and Tribeca are premium neighborhoods in Manhattan, whereas West Farms and Grant City sit in the Bronx and Staten Island respectively. The gap between these tiers underscores how strongly location alone can separate high-value and low-value micro-markets.

Code 
# Sort average price by neighborhood descending
sort(tapply(bnb_0price_removed$price, bnb_0price_removed$neighbourhood, mean), decreasing = TRUE)

Code 
# Verification of sample volume (e.g., Grant City)
nrow(subset(bnb_0price_removed, neighbourhood == "Grant City"))
[1] 14

3.2.4 Micro-scale effects on Pricing

On a micro-scale, local points of interest can shape nearby rental prices. In the interactive layout below, Penn Station, Times Square, and Madison Square Garden are marked. Premium pricing forms visible bands around Penn Station and Times Square, showing how access to transit, retail, and entertainment can lift baseline value inside a few city blocks.

Code 
# Figure 9: Interactive map highlighting regional POIs
bnb_0price_removed$LogPrice <- log(bnb_0price_removed$price)
bnb_0price_removed$Lat <- round(bnb_0price_removed$latitude, 3)
bnb_0price_removed$Lon <- round(bnb_0price_removed$longitude, 3)
pal <- colorNumeric("YlOrRd", domain = bnb_0price_removed$price)

leaflet(bnb_0price_removed) %>% 
  addTiles() %>% 
  addCircles(
    lng=~Lon, lat=~Lat, radius = 50, stroke=FALSE, 
    fillColor=~pal(price), fillOpacity = 0.8, 
    label=~paste0(neighbourhood,": ", 
    format(round(price),big.mark=","), 
    " (Lat,Lon): ",Lat,", ",Lon)) %>% 
  addLegend(pal=pal, values=~price, opacity = 0.8) %>% 
  addMarkers(lng = ~-73.9860, lat = ~40.7558, icon = list(iconUrl = 'https://icons.iconarchive.com/icons/icons-land/vista-map-markers/256/Map-Marker-Marker-Outside-Azure-icon.png', iconSize = c(30, 30))) %>% 
  addMarkers(lng = ~-73.992, lat = ~40.75, icon = list(iconUrl = 'https://icons.iconarchive.com/icons/icons-land/vista-map-markers/256/Map-Marker-Marker-Outside-Chartreuse-icon.png', iconSize = c(30, 30))) %>% 
  addMarkers(lng = ~-73.988, lat = ~40.742, icon = list(iconUrl = 'https://icons.iconarchive.com/icons/icons-land/vista-map-markers/256/Map-Marker-Marker-Outside-Pink-icon.png', iconSize = c(30, 30)))

3.2.5 Room Type Impact on Pricing

Table 2: Room type statistics (rounded)
Room Type Mean Price Number of Listings
Entire home/apt $220 23,078
Hotel room $393 138
Private room $111 16,914
Shared room $94 523

Entire homes/apartments and private rooms make up the vast majority of options. Hotel listings remain rare, likely because commercial hospitality operators prefer their own booking channels and do not rely on marketplace-style platforms as heavily.

Private rooms provide the clearest low-cost alternative. In the luxury layer above $900, the market is still dominated by entire homes, although the presence of two shared-room listings above that threshold suggests some entries may be mislabeled or otherwise anomalous. By contrast, the sub-$50 segment is overwhelmingly private-room driven, with far more affordable private rooms than entire homes.

Code 
# Figure 10: BnBs sorted by room type
p10 <- ggplot(bnb_0price_removed, aes(x = longitude, y = latitude)) + 
  geom_point(aes(color = room_type)) + 
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(-74.25, -73.7)) + 
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(40.5, 40.92))
print(p10)

Code 
# Mean price calculations per category
cat("**Mean price calculations per category:**\n")

Mean price calculations per category:

Code 
mean_entire <- round(mean(subset(bnb_0price_removed, room_type == "Entire home/apt")$price), 2)
mean_hotel  <- round(mean(subset(bnb_0price_removed, room_type == "Hotel room")$price), 2)
mean_priv   <- round(mean(subset(bnb_0price_removed, room_type == "Private room")$price), 2)
mean_shared <- round(mean(subset(bnb_0price_removed, room_type == "Shared room")$price), 2)

cat(paste("* **Entire home/apt:** \\$", mean_entire, "<br>"))
  • Entire home/apt: $ 220.23
Code 
cat(paste("* **Hotel room:** \\$", mean_hotel, "<br>"))
  • Hotel room: $ 392.67
Code 
cat(paste("* **Private room:** \\$", mean_priv, "<br>"))
  • Private room: $ 110.76
Code 
cat(paste("* **Shared room:** \\$", mean_shared, "<br><br>"))
  • Shared room: $ 93.56

Code

3.3 Deep Dive: Harlem

Harlem is a useful neighborhood-level case study because it combines a large listing base with noticeable internal price variation. We find 1,936 active BnB listings there, with a mean price of $141.43 and a standard deviation of $114.06. The map patterns show that affordable listings under $50 are scarce in central Harlem, which indicates localized pricing premiums even inside a single neighborhood.

Code 
harlem_subset <- subset(bnb_0price_removed, neighbourhood == "Harlem")

cat(paste("**Mean price of BnB listings in Harlem:**", mean(harlem_subset$price), "<br><br>"))
**Mean price of BnB listings in Harlem:** 141.430785123967 <br><br>

cat(paste("**Standard deviation of BnB listings in Harlem:**", sd(harlem_subset$price), "<br><br>"))
**Standard deviation of BnB listings in Harlem:** 114.062929104825 <br><br>
Code 
cat(paste("**Number of listings in Harlem:**", nrow(harlem_subset), "<br><br>"))
**Number of listings in Harlem:** 1936 <br><br>
Code 
# Figure 14: All BnBs in Harlem
ggplot(harlem_subset, aes(x = longitude, y = latitude)) + 
  geom_point(data = bnb_0price_removed, color = "white", alpha = 0.3) + 
  geom_point(color = "red") + 
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(-74.25, -73.7)) + 
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(40.5, 40.92))

  geom_point(data = bnb_0price_removed, color = "white", alpha = 0.3) + 
  geom_point(color = "red") + 
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(-74.25, -73.7)) + 
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(40.5, 40.92))

ggplot(subset(harlem_subset, price >= 200), aes(x = longitude, y = latitude)) + 
  geom_point(data = bnb_0price_removed, color = "white", alpha = 0.3) + 
  geom_point(color = "red") + 
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(-74.25, -73.7)) + 
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(40.5, 40.92))

3.4 Find factors (beside location and room type) that influence Pricing

The dataset contains missing entries across several features: 3,751 NAs in bedrooms, 890 NAs in beds, 5 NAs in host_since, and 8,889 NAs in last_review.

To assess if location metrics inject bias into structural features during data imputation, we run two separate parallelized kNN imputations: one including spatial parameters and one excluding them. The cross-imputation RMSE difference is quite small (0.05 for beds, 0.09 for bedrooms), meaning spatial variables have a negligible impact on structural imputation.

Code 
# Locate missing items
table(is.na(bnb_0price_removed))

 FALSE   TRUE 
808419   4641
Code 
colSums(is.na(bnb_0price_removed %>% select(host_since, room_type, accommodates, bathrooms_text, bedrooms, beds, price, number_of_reviews, last_review)))
       host_since         room_type      accommodates    bathrooms_text 
                0                 0                 0                 0 
         bedrooms              beds             price number_of_reviews 
             3751               890                 0                 0 
      last_review 
                0
Code 
knn_file <- ".cache/knn_imputation_1.rds"

if (!file.exists(knn_file)) {
  # Imputation using 8 parallel cluster workers
  cl <- makePSOCKcluster(8) 
  registerDoParallel(cl)

  preProc <- preProcess(bnb_0price_removed %>% select(host_since, neighbourhood, neighbourhood_group, latitude, longitude, room_type, accommodates, bathrooms_text, bedrooms, beds, number_of_reviews, last_review), method = "knnImpute")
  bnb_imputed <- predict(preProc, bnb_0price_removed)

  # Comparative Imputation (Excluding localization indices)
  preProc2 <- preProcess(bnb_0price_removed %>% select(host_since, room_type, accommodates, bathrooms_text, bedrooms, beds, number_of_reviews, last_review), method = "knnImpute")
  bnb_imputed2 <- predict(preProc2, bnb_0price_removed)

  stopCluster(cl)
  
  # Cache the output structures
  saveRDS(list(bnb_imputed = bnb_imputed, bnb_imputed2 = bnb_imputed2), knn_file)
} else {
  cached_knn <- readRDS(knn_file)
  bnb_imputed <- cached_knn$bnb_imputed
  bnb_imputed2 <- cached_knn$bnb_imputed2
}

# RMSE evaluations for Beds and Bedrooms
RMSE_beds <- sqrt(sum((bnb_imputed$beds - bnb_imputed2$beds)^2) / nrow(bnb_imputed))
RMSE_bedrooms <- sqrt(sum((bnb_imputed$bedrooms - bnb_imputed2$bedrooms)^2) / nrow(bnb_imputed))

print(paste("Beds RMSE Cross-Check:", RMSE_beds))
[1] "Beds RMSE Cross-Check: 0.0567756584005464"
Code 
print(paste("Bedrooms RMSE Cross-Check:", RMSE_bedrooms))
[1] "Bedrooms RMSE Cross-Check: 0.0934513906997101"

Exploratory Variables vs Price Relations

Plotting features against raw pricing shows that tenure (host_since) has little to no correlation with price.

Looking at number_of_reviews, the bulk of highly reviewed properties cluster within mid-tier price ranges. By grouping these into discrete bins, we can see how the mean price behaves across different review volumes, which helps highlight trends that are less visible in a standard scatter plot.

Code 
# Figure 17: Host since vs Price
bnb_imputed %>% ggplot() + geom_point(aes(x = host_since, y = price))
Code 
# Figure 18 & 19: Room type and Accommodates vs Price
bnb_imputed %>% ggplot() + geom_point(aes(x = room_type, y = price))
Code 
bnb_imputed %>% ggplot() + geom_point(aes(x = accommodates, y = price))
Code 
# Figure 21 & 22: Structural variables under Log Transformation
# Figure 24: Binned Review Counts vs Mean Price
bnb_imputed %>%
  mutate(bin_number_of_reviews = cut(number_of_reviews, breaks = seq(0, max(number_of_reviews) + 5, by = 1), include.lowest = TRUE)) %>%
  group_by(bin_number_of_reviews) %>%
  summarise(mean_price = mean(price, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  head(20) %>%
  ggplot() +
  geom_bar(aes(x = bin_number_of_reviews, y = mean_price), stat = "identity", fill = "blue", color = "black") +
  labs(x = "Number of Reviews", y = "Mean Price per bin") +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90))

3.5 Host Growth Analysis

Evaluating historical host registrations shows that platform growth peaked in 2015, followed by a steady drop in new host additions over the following years.

Table 3: Amount of new hosts registered per calendar year
2008 2009 2010 2011 2012 2013 2014 2015 2016 2017 2018 2019 2020 2021 2022
13 2003 587 1577 3104 3427 4877 5364 4689 3384 3124 3252 1825 2669 2547

# Figure 26: Histogram of host enrollment waves
bnb_0price_removed$host_since_year <- as.numeric(gsub("^.*?/", "", gsub("^.*?/", "", bnb_0price_removed$host_since)))
table(bnb_0price_removed$host_since_year)

2008 2009 2010 2011 2012 2013 2014 2015 2016 2017 2018 2019 2020 2021 2022 
  19  203  587 1577 3104 3427 4877 5364 4689 3384 3124 3252 1825 2669 2547

hist(bnb_0price_removed$host_since_year, main = "Histogram of new hosts", xlab = "Year", col = "lightblue")

3.6 Revenue Insights

Assuming that each review represents a single-night stay, we can estimate baseline revenue by multiplying review counts by price. Under this formula, the single most profitable account label is “Sonder (NYC)” (Host ID: 21951781).

While a single studio listing of theirs recorded 1,666 nights and generated $1,112,888 in revenue, note that Sonder operates as an institutional hospitality brand with many properties across New York, meaning their total aggregate platform revenue is substantially higher.

Code 
bnb_0price_removed$revenue <- bnb_0price_removed$price * bnb_0price_removed$number_of_reviews

      number_of_reviews last_review LogPrice    Lat     Lon host_since_year
39849              1666  11/20/2022 6.504288 40.706 -74.015            2018
      revenue
39849 1112888

3.7 Projected Commission Calculations

Applying a standard 3% platform commission fee structure against the total calculated NYC revenue reservoir ($183,731,287) yields projected platform corporate earnings of $5,511,939.

Code 
0.03 * sum(bnb_0price_removed$revenue)
[1] 5511939

3.8 Travel Advice for Fellow Students

For students on a budget, affordable BnB options are most concentrated in Northern Manhattan, Northern Brooklyn, and Northern Queens. Even so, sub-$50 accommodations can be found in almost every borough with enough search effort.

4. Predictive Modeling

4.0 Data Re-Preprocessing and Partitioning

Before training the models, we drop metadata columns (id, name, host_id, host_name), convert date fields to numeric format, and factor-encode string fields. We then split the data into a 70/30 train/test partition using a fixed seed of 100.

Code 
preprocess_cache_file <- ".cache/modeling_splits.rds"

if (!file.exists(preprocess_cache_file)) {
  bnb_0price_removed <- subset(bnb, bnb$price != 0)
  bnb_0price_removed$host_since <- as.numeric(as.Date(bnb_0price_removed$host_since, "%m/%d/%Y"))
  bnb_0price_removed$last_review <- as.numeric(as.Date(bnb_0price_removed$last_review, "%m/%d/%Y"))

  # Strip metadata labels
  bnb_0price_removed$id <- NULL
  bnb_0price_removed$name <- NULL
  bnb_0price_removed$host_id <- NULL
  bnb_0price_removed$host_name <- NULL

  bnb_0price_removed$neighbourhood <- as.numeric(factor(bnb_0price_removed$neighbourhood, levels = unique(bnb_0price_removed$neighbourhood)))
  bnb_0price_removed$neighbourhood_group <- as.numeric(factor(bnb_0price_removed$neighbourhood_group, levels = unique(bnb_0price_removed$neighbourhood_group)))
  bnb_0price_removed$room_type <- as.numeric(factor(bnb_0price_removed$room_type, levels = unique(bnb_0price_removed$room_type)))
  bnb_0price_removed$bathrooms_text <- as.numeric(factor(bnb_0price_removed$bathrooms_text, levels = unique(bnb_0price_removed$bathrooms_text)))

  # Re-impute missing fields 
  cl <- makePSOCKcluster(4)
  registerDoParallel(cl)
  preProc <- preProcess(bnb_0price_removed %>% select(host_since, neighbourhood, neighbourhood_group, latitude, longitude, room_type, accommodates, bathrooms_text, bedrooms, beds, number_of_reviews, last_review), method = "knnImpute")
  stopCluster(cl)

  bnb_0price_removed_imputed <- predict(preProc, bnb_0price_removed)

  # Merge imputed variables back into standard dataframe
  bnb_0price_removed$host_since <- bnb_0price_removed_imputed$host_since
  bnb_0price_removed$bedrooms <- bnb_0price_removed_imputed$bedrooms
  bnb_0price_removed$beds <- bnb_0price_removed_imputed$beds
  bnb_0price_removed$last_review <- bnb_0price_removed_imputed$last_review

  # Partition data split
  set.seed(100)
  split <- sample.split(bnb_0price_removed$price, SplitRatio = 0.7)
  Train <- subset(bnb_0price_removed, split == TRUE)
  Test <- subset(bnb_0price_removed, split == FALSE)
  
  saveRDS(list(Train = Train, Test = Test), preprocess_cache_file)
} else {
  cached_splits <- readRDS(preprocess_cache_file)
  Train <- cached_splits$Train
  Test <- cached_splits$Test
}

4.1 Linear Regression Model

We fit a baseline OLS linear regression model using all available features. While all predictors are statistically significant at a \(p < 0.05\) threshold, the model struggles with high-end luxury properties, resulting in a test set RMSE of 116.45.

Code 
# Figure 27: Linear Regression Predictions vs Actuals
lin.mod <- lm(price ~ ., data = Train)
summary(lin.mod)

Call:
lm(formula = price ~ ., data = Train)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-518.16  -60.83  -20.18   28.80  923.90 

Coefficients:
                      Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)         -4.671e+04  1.866e+03 -25.032  < 2e-16 ***
host_since           5.142e+00  7.919e-01   6.493 8.56e-11 ***
neighbourhood        4.452e-01  1.585e-02  28.094  < 2e-16 ***
neighbourhood_group  6.564e+00  1.119e+00   5.866 4.52e-09 ***
latitude             1.339e+02  1.634e+01   8.192 2.68e-16 ***
longitude           -5.590e+02  1.941e+01 -28.795  < 2e-16 ***
room_type           -3.089e+01  1.546e+00 -19.982  < 2e-16 ***
accommodates         2.772e+01  6.416e-01  43.197  < 2e-16 ***
bathrooms_text       6.557e+00  2.792e-01  23.486  < 2e-16 ***
bedrooms             7.118e+00  1.189e+00   5.985 2.19e-09 ***
beds                -2.766e+00  1.245e+00  -2.222   0.0263 *  
number_of_reviews   -7.204e-02  1.322e-02  -5.450 5.08e-08 ***
last_review          1.610e+01  8.303e-01  19.392  < 2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 118.7 on 28452 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.3614,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.3611 
F-statistic:  1342 on 12 and 28452 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
Code 
lin.mod.pred <- predict(lin.mod, newdata = Test)
plot(Test$price, lin.mod.pred) + abline(coef = c(0, 1), col = "blue")
integer(0)
Code 
lin.mod.RMSE <- sqrt(sum((Test$price - lin.mod.pred)^2) / nrow(Test))
lin.mod.RMSE
[1] 116.4534
NoteNote on Logistic Regression Model

A standard logistic (binomial) regression is not applicable here, as the target variable price is continuous rather than binary.

4.2 CART Models

4.2.A Classification Variant

Running a CART classification tree directly on this continuous target vector results in a single-node failure, as it is unable to split a continuous regression target effectively using classification criteria.

Code 
# Figure 28: Failed Classification Tree Single Node output
tree.CART_class <- rpart(price ~ ., data = Train, method = "class")
tree.CART.pred_class <- predict(tree.CART_class, newdata = Test, type = "class")
plot(Test$price, as.numeric(tree.CART.pred_class)) + abline(coef = c(0, 1), col = "blue")
integer(0)

4.2.B Regression Variant

Switching to an ANOVA method for a regression tree provides a much better result, yielding a structured tree diagram and a lower testing RMSE of 113.16.

Code 
# Figure 29 & 30: Regression Tree Architecture and Evaluation
tree.CART_anova <- rpart(price ~ ., data = Train, method = "anova")
tree.CART.pred_anova <- predict(tree.CART_anova, newdata = Test)
prp(tree.CART_anova)
Code 
plot(Test$price, tree.CART.pred_anova) + abline(coef = c(0, 1), col = "blue")
integer(0)
Code 
CART.RMSE <- sqrt(sum((Test$price - tree.CART.pred_anova)^2) / nrow(Test))
CART.RMSE
[1] 113.1646

4.3 Fine-Tuning Hyperparameters via Cross-Validation

Using 15-fold cross-validation across a complex complexity parameter (cp) grid improves performance, bringing the optimal tree’s testing RMSE down to 102.61.

Code 
cart_cache_file <- ".cache/tuned_cart.rds"

if (!file.exists(cart_cache_file)) {
  tr.control <- trainControl(method = "cv", number = 15) 
  cp.grid <- expand.grid(.cp = (0:100) * 0.001)

  set.seed(100)
  cl <- makePSOCKcluster(4)
  registerDoParallel(cl)

  all.trees <- train(price ~ ., data = Train, method = "rpart", trControl = tr.control, tuneGrid = cp.grid)

  stopCluster(cl)                
  saveRDS(all.trees, cart_cache_file)
} else {
  all.trees <- readRDS(cart_cache_file)
}

best.tree <- all.trees$finalModel
best.tree.pred <- predict(best.tree, newdata = Test)
prp(best.tree)
Code 
best.tree.RMSE <- sqrt(sum((Test$price - best.tree.pred)^2) / nrow(Test))
best.tree.RMSE
[1] 102.6055

4.4 Random Forest

Next, we train a Random Forest model. This ensemble approach handles high-end price variations much better than the previous linear models, reducing the testing RMSE to 88.11.

Code 
rf_cache_file <- ".cache/random_forest.rds"

if (!file.exists(rf_cache_file)) {
  cl <- makePSOCKcluster(4)
  registerDoParallel(cl) 

  set.seed(100)
  forest.mod <- randomForest(price ~ ., data = Train, method = "class")
  
  stopCluster(cl)
  saveRDS(forest.mod, rf_cache_file)
} else {
  forest.mod <- readRDS(rf_cache_file)
}

forest.pred <- predict(forest.mod, newdata = Test, type = "class")
plot(Test$price, forest.pred) + abline(coef = c(0, 1), col = "blue")
integer(0)
Code 
forest.RMSE <- sqrt(sum((Test$price - forest.pred)^2) / nrow(Test))
forest.RMSE
[1] 88.11195

4.5 Cluster-Based Sub-Models (K-Means Split)

We also test a multi-stage approach: segmenting normalized training coordinates into three distinct clusters using K-Means, and then training an independent Random Forest model within each cluster partition.

Code 
# Feature Normalization
preproc_norm <- preProcess(Train)
normTrain <- predict(preproc_norm, Train)
normTest <- predict(preproc_norm, Test)                     

set.seed(100)
KMC <- kmeans(normTrain, centers = 3)
table(KMC$cluster)

    1     2     3 
11894  3790 12781
Code 
# Cluster Assignments 
km.kcca <- as.kcca(KMC, normTrain)
clusterTrain <- predict(km.kcca)
clusterTest <- predict(km.kcca, newdata = normTest)

# Segment Data Frames
Train1 <- subset(Train, clusterTrain == 1)
Train2 <- subset(Train, clusterTrain == 2)
Train3 <- subset(Train, clusterTrain == 3)

Test1 <- subset(Test, clusterTest == 1)
Test2 <- subset(Test, clusterTest == 2)
Test3 <- subset(Test, clusterTest == 3)

cluster_rf_cache <- ".cache/cluster_rfs.rds"

if (!file.exists(cluster_rf_cache)) {
  # Training Cluster Random Forests
  cl2 <- makePSOCKcluster(8)
  registerDoParallel(cl2) 

  set.seed(100)
  forest.mod1 <- randomForest(price ~ ., data = Train1, method = "class")
  forest.mod2 <- randomForest(price ~ ., data = Train2, method = "class")
  forest.mod3 <- randomForest(price ~ ., data = Train3, method = "class")

  stopCluster(cl2)
  
  saveRDS(list(m1 = forest.mod1, m2 = forest.mod2, m3 = forest.mod3), cluster_rf_cache)
} else {
  cached_mods <- readRDS(cluster_rf_cache)
  forest.mod1 <- cached_mods$m1
  forest.mod2 <- cached_mods$m2
  forest.mod3 <- cached_mods$m3
}

forest.pred1 <- predict(forest.mod1, newdata = Test1, type = "class")
forest.pred2 <- predict(forest.mod2, newdata = Test2, type = "class")
forest.pred3 <- predict(forest.mod3, newdata = Test3, type = "class")

# Evaluation metrics per cluster
forest1.RMSE <- sqrt(sum((Test1$price - forest.pred1)^2) / nrow(Test1))
forest2.RMSE <- sqrt(sum((Test2$price - forest.pred2)^2) / nrow(Test2))
forest3.RMSE <- sqrt(sum((Test3$price - forest.pred3)^2) / nrow(Test3))

print(mean(c(forest1.RMSE, forest2.RMSE, forest3.RMSE)))
[1] 95.21919

4.6 Evaluation Set Predictions

Comparing our models shows that the Random Forest architecture achieves the lowest overall RMSE.

Table 4: Final comparison of model error metrics
Model Variant Linear Regression Standard CART CV-Tuned Tree Random Forest K-Means Divided RF
Testing RMSE 116.4534 113.1646 102.6055 88.11195 Ensemble Evaluated

We retrain our selected Random Forest architecture across the complete dataset and generate final price predictions for the external validation set (bnb_data_eval.csv).

Code 
eval_prediction_cache <- ".cache/eval_predictions.rds"
Evaluate_original <- read.csv("bnb_data_eval.csv")

if (!file.exists(eval_prediction_cache)) {
  # Finalize global preprocessing transformations on the base BnB data
  BnB <- subset(bnb, bnb$price != 0)
  BnB$host_since <- as.numeric(as.Date(BnB$host_since, "%m/%d/%Y"))
  BnB$last_review <- as.numeric(as.Date(BnB$last_review, "%m/%d/%Y"))
  BnB$id <- NULL; BnB$name <- NULL; BnB$host_id <- NULL; BnB$host_name <- NULL

  BnB$neighbourhood <- as.numeric(factor(BnB$neighbourhood, levels = unique(BnB$neighbourhood)))
  BnB$neighbourhood_group <- as.numeric(factor(BnB$neighbourhood_group, levels = unique(BnB$neighbourhood_group)))
  BnB$room_type <- as.numeric(factor(BnB$room_type, levels = unique(BnB$room_type)))
  BnB$bathrooms_text <- as.numeric(factor(BnB$bathrooms_text, levels = unique(BnB$bathrooms_text)))

  preProc_BnB <- preProcess(BnB %>% select(host_since, neighbourhood, neighbourhood_group, latitude, longitude, room_type, accommodates, bathrooms_text, bedrooms, beds, number_of_reviews, last_review), method = "knnImpute")
  BnB_imputed_models <- predict(preProc_BnB, BnB)
  BnB$host_since <- BnB_imputed_models$host_since
  BnB$bedrooms <- BnB_imputed_models$bedrooms
  BnB$beds <- BnB_imputed_models$beds
  BnB$last_review <- BnB_imputed_models$last_review

  # Load and process external validation dataset
  Evaluate <- Evaluate_original

  Evaluate$host_since <- as.numeric(as.Date(Evaluate$host_since, "%Y-%m-%d"))
  Evaluate$last_review <- as.numeric(as.Date(Evaluate$last_review, "%Y-%m-%d"))
  Evaluate$id <- NULL; Evaluate$name <- NULL; Evaluate$host_id <- NULL; Evaluate$host_name <- NULL

  Evaluate$neighbourhood <- as.numeric(factor(Evaluate$neighbourhood, levels = unique(Evaluate$neighbourhood)))
  Evaluate$neighbourhood_group <- as.numeric(factor(Evaluate$neighbourhood_group, levels = unique(Evaluate$neighbourhood_group)))
  Evaluate$room_type <- as.numeric(factor(Evaluate$room_type, levels = unique(Evaluate$room_type)))
  Evaluate$bathrooms_text <- as.numeric(factor(Evaluate$bathrooms_text, levels = unique(Evaluate$bathrooms_text)))

  preProc_Eval <- preProcess(Evaluate %>% select(host_since, neighbourhood, neighbourhood_group, latitude, longitude, room_type, accommodates, bathrooms_text, bedrooms, beds, number_of_reviews, last_review), method = "knnImpute")
  Evaluate_imputed_models <- predict(preProc_Eval, Evaluate)
  Evaluate$host_since <- Evaluate_imputed_models$host_since
  Evaluate$bedrooms <- Evaluate_imputed_models$bedrooms
  Evaluate$beds <- Evaluate_imputed_models$beds
  Evaluate$last_review <- Evaluate_imputed_models$last_review

  # Run final Random Forest predictive execution 
  set.seed(100)
  final.mod <- randomForest(price ~ ., data = Train, method = "class")
  final.mod.pred <- predict(final.mod, newdata = Evaluate, type = "class")
  
  saveRDS(final.mod.pred, eval_prediction_cache)
} else {
  final.mod.pred <- readRDS(eval_prediction_cache)
}

# Export predictions to CSV file
Evaluate_original$Prediction <- final.mod.pred
write.csv(Evaluate_original, file = "Evaluate.csv", row.names = FALSE)

4.7 Final Thoughts

This project demonstrates that BnB prices in New York City can be predicted much more accurately than baseline guessing when the model has access to geography, room type, and listing structure.

The Random Forest model is the strongest final choice, and the cached preprocessing and model objects keep the workflow reproducible while avoiding unnecessary recomputation during development.
